pages.text: With viruses running rampant and zombies roaming the streets, you might feel reassured as you sit at home, admiring your food-stocked pantry and fully loaded freezer. Hopefully, it’s all canned food—because if the power goes out, keeping your food fresh will quickly become your biggest headache.

Freezers are energy guzzlers, and while energy is already as precious as gold in peacetime, it will be even more so in an apocalyptic scenario. That’s why it’s best to preserve your food using alternative methods such as dehydration,

salting, or vacuum sealing. Now is the time—hurry and buy a vacuum sealer! Ideally, choose a battery-powered model so you can stockpile batteries now, ensuring you have enough energy when the power fails. And it’s no coincidence that battery-powered vacuum sealers are trending on the market; they really know how to do business. Let’s just hope that the profits they rake in will still be of any use when the world comes crashing down.

$(o)(There is a manual attached here)$()$(br)$(#6F6F6F)$(l)Glair GRE-738 Food Vacuum Sealer$()$(br)$(#6F6F6F)Specifications$(li)Power: 580W$(li)Sealing Material: Standard plastic bags$(li)Body Material: Food-grade alloy$(li)Vacuum Degree: 80KPA$(li)Power Source: High-capacity battery$(br)Usage Notes$(li)One standard high-capacity battery can seal ~500 food
$(#6F6F6F)portions. Replenish power as needed.$(li)Do not leave food in the chamber without sealing—prolonged exposure risks spoilage and machine damage.
